title: Your first loop
instruction:
Think about a washing machine. You do not press "wash" 10 times for 10 items of clothing. You load everything, press start, and it repeats the same action until the job is done.

A loop does the same thing. Write the action once. Tell it how many times to repeat.

Run this. It prints five numbers without you writing five Println lines.
successMessage: Five lines of output. One loop. That is the whole point.

title: The three parts of a for loop
instruction:
A `for` loop has three parts separated by semicolons:

`for start; condition; step`

- `i := 1` — start here
- `i <= 5` — keep going while this is true
- `i++` — after each loop, do this

`i++` means add 1 to i. It is shorthand for `i = i + 1`.

Change the loop to count from 1 to 10.
hint: Change i <= 5 to i <= 10. Leave the rest the same.

codeChecks.message: Change the condition to i <= 10 to count up to 10.

successMessage: Start. Condition. Step. Three parts, infinite possibilities.

title: Use the counter inside the loop
instruction:
`i` is just a variable. You can use it inside the loop body — multiply it, add to it, print it in a sentence.

Add a line inside the loop that prints: `"2 x [i] = [result]"`

So the output should look like:
```
2 x 1 = 2
2 x 2 = 4
2 x 3 = 6
```
hint: fmt.Printf("2 x %d = %d\n", i, 2*i)  — use the loop counter directly in the calculation.

codeChecks.message: Use fmt.Printf to format the output.
Multiply i by 2 to get the result: 2*i
Replace the TODO comment with your Printf line.

successMessage: The counter is a real variable. Use it in any expression you want.

title: Counting down
instruction:
Loops do not have to count up. Change the step and direction and they count down.

`i--` means subtract 1 from i each time (opposite of `i++`).

Change this loop to count down from 5 to 1.
hint: Start at 5, condition i >= 1, step i--

codeChecks.message: Use i-- to count down instead of up.

successMessage: Same structure, opposite direction. i-- is your reverse gear.

title: while-style loop
successMessage: 1 doubles to 2, 4, 8... and hits 128 which breaks the condition. That is a while loop in Go.

title: Infinite loop with break
instruction:
`for { }` with no condition runs forever. That sounds terrifying, but it is actually useful — when you do not know in advance how many times to loop.

You control when it stops using `break`.

This loop counts up and breaks when it hits 5. Add a line that prints `"Done"` after the loop ends.
hint: Add fmt.Println("Done") after the closing brace of the for loop — not inside it.

codeChecks.message: Replace the TODO comment with your Println line.

successMessage: break jumps out of the loop immediately. The code after the loop keeps running normally.

title: continue — skip and keep going
instruction:
`continue` skips the rest of the current iteration and jumps straight to the next one. The loop keeps running — just not for that particular round.

This loop prints numbers 1 to 10 but skips even numbers. Change it to skip odd numbers instead (print only even numbers).
hint: Change the condition from i%2 == 0 to i%2 != 0 — skip odd numbers, let even ones through.

codeChecks.message: Skip odd numbers by checking i%2 != 0

successMessage: continue skips. break stops. Two different tools for controlling what happens inside a loop.

title: Nested loops — a loop inside a loop
instruction:
You can put a loop inside another loop. The inner loop runs completely for every single iteration of the outer loop.

This prints a 3x3 grid of coordinates. Run it and see the pattern.

Then change both loops to go from 1 to 4 instead of 1 to 3.
hint: Change both i <= 3 and j <= 3 to i <= 4 and j <= 4.

codeChecks.message: Change the outer loop to go up to 4.
Change the inner loop to go up to 4.

successMessage: 4 outer iterations x 4 inner iterations = 16 total. Nested loops multiply.

title: Running total
instruction:
One of the most common loop patterns: start a variable at zero, add to it inside the loop, read the result after.

This adds up numbers 1 to 5. Extend it to add up numbers 1 to 100 and print the total.
hint: Change i <= 5 to i <= 100. The rest stays the same.

codeChecks.message: Change the loop to go up to 100.

successMessage: 1+2+3+...+100 = 5050. Carl Friedrich Gauss figured this out as a child. Now you can verify it in three seconds.

title: range — cleaner counting
instruction:
When you just want to count a fixed number of times, `range` is a cleaner option.

`for i := range 5` counts 0, 1, 2, 3, 4 — it gives you 5 iterations starting from zero.

Run this. Then change it to print `"Step 1"` through `"Step 5"` by adding 1 to i in the Printf.
hint: fmt.Printf("Step %d\n", i+1)  — add 1 to i because range starts at 0.

codeChecks.message: Add 1 to i so the output starts at 1 instead of 0: fmt.Printf("Step %d\n", i+1)

successMessage: range 5 gives you 5 clean iterations. Starts at 0, which is the Go convention — you will see this everywhere.

title: Fix the infinite loop
instruction:
This program is supposed to print numbers until it reaches 10 — but it runs forever and never stops.

Run it. You will see it keeps going (click Stop to halt it). Then find the bug and fix it.
hint: Look at what happens to i inside the loop. Is it ever getting closer to the condition being false?

codeChecks.message: Change i-- to i++ so the counter moves toward the condition.

successMessage: i-- was counting down, so i <= 10 was always true. Flip to i++ and it counts toward the exit. Classic bug — now you know to look for it.

title: Build a countdown
instruction:
No starter code.

Write a program that:
1. Counts down from 10 to 1, printing each number
2. Skips the number 5 using `continue`
3. Prints `"Liftoff!"` after the loop ends
hint: for i := 10; i >= 1; i-- { if i == 5 { continue } fmt.Println(i) }  — then fmt.Println("Liftoff!") after the loop.

codeChecks.message: Use continue to skip the number 5.

successMessage: Countdown built. You used a for loop, i--, continue, and a post-loop print. That is a full chapter in one program.
